findings.summary: Firezone (VPN security app) chose Tauri for Windows and Linux GUI clients because their core library connlib was already Rust. Key architectural decision: separated Tauri GUI process from connlib via IPC, putting a boundary between web engine code and cryptography code. This was a security win (systemd sandboxing) and also a convenience win (run without admin privileges). Architecture was copied to Windows where it coincidentally fixed a DNS control deactivation bug.
lessons: IPC separation between GUI and security-critical code is a production-proven pattern
Tauri's Rust backend makes security isolation natural
Sandboxing model differs between Linux (systemd, strict) and Windows (less strict) -- plan for platform differences
Same architecture can yield unexpected bug fixes on different platforms
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Canvas Learning System also needs IPC between frontend and backend services (Docker containers). The security boundary pattern is directly applicable.

findings.summary: Evil Martians built a desktop app with Tauri + sidecar, porting features from a preexisting web app while adding filesystem capabilities (file change monitoring). Framework provides productive development flow by catching many errors at compile time. Sidecar can be written in any language, running alongside the desktop app as a background process.
lessons: Sidecar pattern enables polyglot architecture -- use the best language for each component
Tauri's compile-time error catching reduces runtime surprises in production
Filesystem watching capabilities work well when ported from web app context
Sidecar process can surpass the framework's built-in capabilities
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Directly validates the sidecar pattern for running Python/Docker backend alongside Tauri frontend.

findings.summary: During Tauri 2.0 RC phase, a security advisory (CVE-2024-35222) required structural changes. The team concluded that reducing public API exposure was necessary before stable release to avoid future breaking changes. Some apps were deployed to production during beta, motivating these improvements.
lessons: Do not deploy Tauri beta/RC to production -- wait for stable releases
Security model changes can require breaking API changes
Capability-based permission system (everything disabled by default) is the correct security posture
findings.relevance_to_canvas: MEDIUM - Reminds us to use stable Tauri 2.0+ and properly configure the permission system.

findings.source: Tauri v2 Auto-Update and Distribution Guide (Oflight)
findings.summary: Production auto-update implementation using tauri-plugin-updater. Automated multi-platform builds, code signing, and GitHub Releases on tag push -- completing release in 5 minutes. Development teams report 70% reduction in user support inquiries with proper auto-update. Signing is mandatory (cannot be disabled).
lessons: Auto-update is critical for desktop apps -- implement from day one
CI/CD pipeline with tauri-action automates cross-platform builds + signing + release
Update signature verification is mandatory in Tauri (security by design)
Static JSON on S3/GitHub Gist or dynamic update server are both viable patterns
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Canvas Learning System will need auto-update for desktop distribution.

findings.source: Ship Your Tauri v2 App Like a Pro (DEV Community)
findings.summary: Two-part guide covering complete release pipeline: code signing certificates, automated cross-platform builds on GitHub Actions, and distribution. Authenticode signing uses signtool.exe, some projects use Azure Key Vault for cloud-based signing.
lessons: macOS requires notarization in addition to code signing
Windows Authenticode signing is needed for trusted installation
Azure Key Vault is viable for team-based signing workflows
GitHub Actions is the standard CI/CD for Tauri cross-platform builds
findings.relevance_to_canvas: MEDIUM - Distribution pipeline reference for future production releases.

findings.summary: UMLBoard migrated their IPC messaging system from Electron/TypeScript to Tauri/Rust. Rust enums provide an elegant and type-safe way to implement message systems. Critical insight: since both Redux and IPC rely on plain serializable JSON objects, everything going through Redux dispatcher can also go through IPC channel -- reuse actions and payloads without additional DTOs.
lessons: Redux actions can be directly reused as IPC messages (JSON serialization compatibility)
Rust enums are superior to TypeScript discriminated unions for IPC message types
Handle serialization errors when converting JSON to Rust enum variants
No need for separate DTO layer if frontend already uses serializable state
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Canvas Learning System will use React state management + Tauri IPC. Redux/Zustand action reuse pattern is directly applicable.

findings.summary: UMLBoard ported local data storage from Electron's nedb (@seald-io/nedb, embedded single-file JSON DB) to Tauri. Used repository trait pattern to decouple application logic from database backend. Evaluated SurrealDB and BonsaiDB as Rust-native alternatives.
lessons: Repository trait pattern enables swapping database backends without changing app logic
SurrealDB and BonsaiDB are viable Rust-native embedded database options
Moving from JavaScript embedded DB (nedb) to Rust DB requires architectural decoupling
Single-file JSON databases work for small data but consider proper embedded DB for growth
findings.relevance_to_canvas: MEDIUM - Canvas data is primarily in Obsidian vault files, but local caching/settings storage will benefit from this pattern.

findings.summary: DoltHub found Tauri APIs fit more naturally in application code vs Electron's always-IPC approach. WebView compatibility issues across OS webviews are exceedingly rare for major operating systems. Backend language difference (Rust vs Node.js) is the main migration hurdle.
lessons: Tauri's command pattern is more natural than Electron's mandatory IPC for everything
Cross-platform WebView compatibility is a non-issue in practice for major OSes
Rust learning curve is the primary barrier -- mitigate by keeping most logic in frontend
Don't need full Rust expertise -- basic command handlers and sidecar management suffice
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Validates that minimal Rust knowledge is sufficient when most logic stays in React frontend.

findings.summary: Hopp (remote pair programming tool) chose Tauri for: ultra-low latency needs (Rust backend), built-in sidecar concept for external binary lifecycle management, and smaller bundle size. Initial Rust compilation is slower but subsequent builds are fast. Tauri's sidecar concept simplified managing screen streaming binary.
lessons: Tauri sidecar concept simplifies external binary lifecycle management
Initial Rust compile times are slow -- use incremental compilation
Rust backend performance is critical for latency-sensitive operations
Bundle size advantage is significant: few MBs vs 150MB+ for Electron
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Validates sidecar pattern for managing external processes. Bundle size advantage matters for user experience.

findings.summary: Real-world application comparison showing Tauri app at ~3MB vs Electron at ~52MB. Performance benchmarks demonstrate significantly faster startup and lower memory usage.
lessons: 17x smaller bundle size in real-world application (3MB vs 52MB)
Startup time under 0.5s vs 1-2s for Electron
Memory usage ~30-40MB idle vs ~200MB for Electron
These advantages compound for user experience and distribution
findings.relevance_to_canvas: MEDIUM - Confirms performance expectations for the migration decision.

findings.summary: Official performance optimization guide covering memoization of custom nodes (React.memo), prop memoization (useMemo/useCallback), avoiding direct access to frequently-changing objects (nodes/edges/viewport), and CSS complexity reduction.
lessons: MUST wrap custom node components in React.memo -- single most important optimization
Never access nodes/edges directly in components that don't need all of them -- use selectors
Store selected nodes separately instead of filtering from nodes array
Complex CSS (animations, shadows, gradients) significantly impacts performance at scale
Enable snapToGrid for fewer state updates during drag operations
findings.relevance_to_canvas: CRITICAL - Canvas Learning System's core UI is a ReactFlow canvas. These optimizations are mandatory.

findings.source: Synergy Codes - Ultimate Guide to Optimize React Flow Performance
findings.summary: Comprehensive ebook covering: lazy rendering (hide nodes initially, render on expand), grid snapping for fewer state updates, CSS optimization for large node counts, and state management patterns that prevent full diagram re-renders.
lessons: Lazy rendering: hide nodes initially, render on expand -- essential for large graphs
Grid snapping reduces state update frequency during drag operations
Bad state management causes entire diagram restarts instead of partial updates
Memoized objects must have stable dependencies -- unstable deps negate memoization
Separate node data from node position state to minimize re-render scope
findings.relevance_to_canvas: CRITICAL - Learning canvas will have many nodes. Lazy rendering and state separation are essential patterns.

findings.source: React Flow Official - State Management with Zustand
findings.summary: React Flow already uses Zustand internally, making it the natural external state management choice. Recommended pattern: put nodes/edges/handlers in Zustand store for basic interactivity. Custom nodes access store actions directly instead of receiving callbacks via data prop.
lessons: Zustand is the natural choice -- React Flow uses it internally
Put nodes, edges, and all change handlers in Zustand store
Custom nodes should access Zustand actions directly, not via data prop callbacks
Keep event handlers (onDrop etc.) in components with ReactFlowProvider context, not in store
Each custom node component wrapped with React.memo() to prevent cascading re-renders
findings.relevance_to_canvas: CRITICAL - Zustand is the recommended state management for React Flow. This should be our default choice.

findings.source: Pinpoint Engineering - Workflow Editor with Auto Layout
findings.summary: Pinpoint Engineering built a production workflow editor. Created useAutoLayout custom hook for automatic layout recalculation on node add/remove/resize/reposition. Canvas stays centered and visually consistent during user interactions. Used Sideload Allowlist for efficient workflow updates.
lessons: useAutoLayout hook pattern for automatic layout management
Canvas centering on structural changes improves UX significantly
Separate layout logic from business logic via custom hooks
Batch workflow updates through allowlist pattern for performance
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Learning canvas needs auto-layout. The useAutoLayout hook pattern is directly reusable.

findings.summary: React Flow is not made for whiteboard applications but can integrate common whiteboard features like drawing capabilities for annotation alongside nodes and edges.
lessons: React Flow is optimized for node-based UIs, not free-form drawing
Whiteboard features (drawing, annotation) are additive, not core
For canvas learning, stick to node-based interaction model -- don't try to make it a whiteboard
Custom nodes can contain any React content, including rich interactive elements
findings.relevance_to_canvas: MEDIUM - Clarifies that Canvas Learning should use node-based paradigm, not whiteboard paradigm.

findings.summary: Practical optimization experience: identified that objects passed to ReactFlow must be memoized with useMemo, functions with useCallback. Direct node/edge access is the #1 performance killer. Store selected nodes in separate state field.
lessons: Direct node/edge access in components is the #1 performance killer
All props to ReactFlow component must be memoized
Separate selection state from node/edge arrays
Use useStore hook with selectors for granular state access
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Practical optimization patterns to follow from day one.

findings.source: Design Systems Collective - Why shadcn/ui Struggles at Enterprise Scale
findings.summary: Front-end engineer explains hidden costs, performance pitfalls, and upkeep burdens of shadcn/ui at enterprise scale. Success depends less on the component library and more on Design Ops (processes + people + tools). Can become backbone of custom design system if properly forked with governance, versioning, and accessibility workflows.
lessons: shadcn/ui at scale requires Design Ops: lint rules, style token mapping, periodic upstream sync
Without governance, copy-paste model accumulates tech debt rapidly
Fork and treat as internal design system, not as a dependency you modify directly
Implement versioning for your copied components to track drift from upstream
Accessibility workflows need explicit attention -- not automatic
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Canvas Learning System should treat shadcn/ui components as internal design system with governance from the start.

findings.source: Internal Orbit - Honest shadcn UI Experience 2025
findings.summary: Dedicated developers just to maintain custom components. Each UI change or bug fix meant updating lots of code and retesting. Missing components (multi-select dropdowns, stepper forms, grouped checkboxes) required building from scratch.
lessons: Budget maintenance time for customized components -- they diverge from upstream
Missing components require building from scratch using Radix primitives
Multi-select, stepper forms, grouped checkboxes are common gaps
Each customization creates a unique maintenance burden not shared by community
findings.relevance_to_canvas: MEDIUM - Be aware of missing components and plan custom development time.

findings.source: OpenReplay - Why Developers Switch to shadcn/ui
findings.summary: Copy-paste architecture gives code ownership but makes maintenance harder. Localized parts differ from upstream, merges are manual, more repo-specific problems and long-term maintenance costs. Performance optimization (code splitting) requires manual effort.
lessons: Code ownership advantage vs maintenance cost is the fundamental trade-off
Manual merge from upstream is a regular maintenance task
Code splitting for shadcn components must be done manually
shadcn excels when you want total control and have time to maintain
findings.relevance_to_canvas: MEDIUM - Accept the trade-off: full control in exchange for maintenance responsibility.

findings.summary: Community discussion on structuring shadcn-ui in large-scale production design systems. Safest way is to treat copied components as your internal design system, not as a dependency you modify directly.
lessons: Create a components/ui/ directory that is treated as an internal library
Never modify copied components in place -- extend via wrapper components
Keep a manifest of which upstream version each component was copied from
Wrapper components allow customization without blocking upstream updates
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Concrete directory structure and wrapper pattern for maintainable shadcn/ui usage.

findings.source: Makers Den - React UI Libraries 2025 Comparison
findings.summary: Comprehensive comparison of React UI libraries. shadcn/ui is excellent for projects needing full control. For smaller teams needing speed, Ant Design or MUI save development time. shadcn is best when you have time to build and maintain components yourself.
lessons: shadcn best for: full control, custom design system, accessibility requirements
Not best for: small teams, rapid prototyping, out-of-box completeness
Radix primitives (shadcn's foundation) are the most accessible in the ecosystem
Consider hybrid: shadcn for core components + pre-built library for complex widgets
findings.relevance_to_canvas: MEDIUM - Validates shadcn choice for Canvas Learning (custom design system) while flagging gaps.

findings.summary: Community discussion on using Tauri to start Docker containers. Key finding: when running docker commands from a built Tauri app, you MUST supply the full path to the Docker executable (e.g., /usr/local/bin/docker) -- relative 'docker' command won't work in built application.
lessons: CRITICAL: Use full path to docker executable in built apps (PATH not inherited)
docker compose --build -d works in dev mode (pnpm tauri dev) but may fail in production build
Must detect Docker installation path at runtime for cross-platform compatibility
Consider docker socket API as alternative to CLI spawning
findings.relevance_to_canvas: CRITICAL - Canvas Learning System needs to manage Docker containers. Full-path requirement is a must-know.

findings.summary: Dockerman is a production Docker management UI built with Tauri + Rust. Provides real-time container stats monitoring, log viewing, container management with detailed inspection and terminal access. Proves the Tauri + Docker management pattern is viable in production.
lessons: Tauri + Docker management is proven in production (Dockerman)
Real-time stats and log streaming are achievable
Container lifecycle management (start/stop/restart/remove) works
Terminal access to containers is possible from Tauri app
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Validates that Tauri can manage Docker containers in production. Dockerman is a reference implementation.

findings.summary: Tauri's sidecar system embeds external binaries with target-triple suffix naming. Requires shell:allow-execute or shell:allow-spawn permissions in capabilities. Frontend spawns via @tauri-apps/plugin-shell. PyInstaller binaries have PID tracking issues (bootloader vs actual process).
lessons: Sidecar binaries need target-triple suffix (e.g., my-sidecar-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
shell:allow-execute for one-shot, shell:allow-spawn for long-running processes
PyInstaller-bundled Python has PID tracking issues -- consider alternatives
Sidecar permissions must be explicitly granted in capabilities/default.json
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - If bundling Python backend as sidecar, be aware of PyInstaller PID issues.

findings.summary: Tauri plugin for managing sidecar processes: spawning, monitoring, health checks, auto-restart, and graceful shutdown. Addresses the gap that Tauri's built-in sidecar support requires significant boilerplate for production-ready lifecycle management.
lessons: Production sidecar management needs: spawn, monitor, health check, auto-restart, graceful shutdown
Tauri's built-in sidecar support is basic -- need wrapper for production use
tauri-sidecar-manager plugin provides production-ready lifecycle management
Zombie process prevention when app closes is a critical concern
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Either use this plugin or implement equivalent lifecycle management for Docker/sidecar processes.

findings.source: GitHub Issue #3062 - Sidecar Lifecycle Management Plugin
findings.summary: Feature request for official Tauri sidecar lifecycle management plugin. Confirms that the community widely needs: spawning, monitoring, health checks, auto-restart, and graceful shutdown for external binaries.
lessons: Official lifecycle management is not yet built into Tauri -- must implement or use community plugin
Health checks for sidecar processes are a common production requirement
Auto-restart on crash is essential for reliability
Graceful shutdown prevents data corruption and zombie processes
findings.relevance_to_canvas: MEDIUM - Confirms the gap and need for custom lifecycle management implementation.

findings.summary: Vite is a first-class frontend build tool for Tauri. Plenty of starter templates available. Since Tauri is frontend-agnostic, Vite's fast HMR and build optimizations apply directly.
lessons: Vite is the recommended build tool for Tauri + React projects
Use official create-tauri-app templates for correct configuration
Vite HMR works seamlessly with Tauri dev mode
Frontend bundle optimization directly impacts Tauri app size (no browser bundled)
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Vite is the confirmed build tool choice for Canvas Learning System.

findings.source: DasRoot Blog - Rust + React/Vue/Tauri Desktop Apps
findings.summary: Modern desktop app architecture combining Rust backend with React/Vue frontend via Tauri. Covers build optimization including tree-shaking, code splitting, lazy loading. Rust-specific optimizations: opt-level='z', LTO, strip symbols can reduce binary by 30-40%.
lessons: Tree-shaking, code splitting, lazy loading -- every KB saved directly impacts download/startup
Rust build optimizations: opt-level='z' + lto=true + strip='symbols' = 30-40% smaller binary
Use terser for frontend minification in production builds
Configure Cargo.toml [profile.release] for production builds specifically
findings.relevance_to_canvas: HIGH - Build optimization checklist for production release pipeline.

findings.summary: Tauri 2.0 supports iOS and Android in addition to desktop. Same React + Vite frontend code can target mobile platforms. Plugin system extends functionality without core modifications.
lessons: Same React codebase can target desktop AND mobile via Tauri 2.0
Plugin system (@tauri-apps/plugin-*) for platform-specific features
Consider mobile from the start -- responsive design pays off later
Platform-conditional code via Tauri's platform detection APIs
findings.relevance_to_canvas: LOW - Mobile support is future consideration, not MVP priority.

findings.source: LogRocket - Build Pomodoro Timer with Tauri + React + Vite
findings.summary: Step-by-step guide for Tauri + React + Vite integration. Covers project setup, IPC commands, state management, and build configuration. Good reference for initial project scaffold.
lessons: Tauri commands should be thin wrappers -- keep logic in frontend when possible
Use invoke() for Rust commands, listen()/emit() for events
Vite config needs specific settings for Tauri: clearScreen: false, server.strictPort: true
TAURI_DEV_HOST env var needed for mobile development
findings.relevance_to_canvas: MEDIUM - Good scaffold reference for initial project setup.

critical_patterns.lesson: Separate heavy backend processes (Docker, Python services) from Tauri GUI via IPC. This is the dominant production pattern. Use sidecar for bundled binaries, Docker for containerized services, localhost HTTP/WebSocket for communication.
critical_patterns.action: Implement Tool-UI Bridge as Tauri command layer that communicates with Docker containers via HTTP/WS

critical_patterns.lesson: React Flow uses Zustand internally. External state should also use Zustand for consistency. Custom nodes access Zustand actions directly. All node/edge data must be memoized.
critical_patterns.action: Use Zustand as the sole state management solution. Wrap all custom nodes in React.memo.

critical_patterns.lesson: Built Tauri apps don't inherit PATH. Must detect and use full path to docker/docker-compose executables. Test in built app, not just dev mode.
critical_patterns.action: Implement Docker path detection at app startup. Fallback to common installation paths per platform.

critical_patterns.lesson: Copy components into components/ui/, never modify in place, extend via wrappers. Maintain upstream version manifest. Budget maintenance time for customizations.
critical_patterns.action: Create components/ui/ directory with wrapper pattern. Track upstream versions in manifest file.

critical_patterns.lesson: Desktop apps without auto-update create massive support overhead. Implement tauri-plugin-updater early. CI/CD with tauri-action automates the entire release pipeline.
critical_patterns.action: Include auto-update in initial architecture. Set up GitHub Actions with tauri-action.

common_pitfalls.description: Accessing nodes/edges directly in components, not memoizing custom nodes, passing unstable references as props
common_pitfalls.mitigation: React.memo on all custom nodes, Zustand selectors for granular access, useMemo/useCallback for all props

common_pitfalls.pitfall: Docker commands fail in built Tauri app
common_pitfalls.description: docker/docker-compose works in dev mode but fails in production build because PATH is not inherited
common_pitfalls.mitigation: Always use absolute paths to Docker executables. Detect at runtime. Test with built app, not just dev.

common_pitfalls.description: Customized components diverge from upstream, making updates painful. No tracking of which version was copied.
common_pitfalls.mitigation: Wrapper pattern (never modify copied files directly). Version manifest. Lint rules for style token consistency.

common_pitfalls.description: Sidecar processes not properly cleaned up when app closes, especially PyInstaller binaries (PID mismatch)
common_pitfalls.mitigation: Implement graceful shutdown, use tauri-sidecar-manager or equivalent, avoid PyInstaller for sidecar binaries

common_pitfalls.description: First Tauri build takes minutes due to Rust compilation. Subsequent builds are fast (incremental).
common_pitfalls.mitigation: Use incremental compilation. Set up CI caching for Rust target/. Developers: expect first build to be slow.

common_pitfalls.description: System WebView may not support latest CSS features uniformly across platforms
common_pitfalls.mitigation: Test on all target platforms. Avoid bleeding-edge CSS. Use Tailwind for consistent cross-platform styling.

recommended_architecture_for_canvas.summary: Based on all blog findings, the recommended architecture is: Tauri 2.0 shell with React 19 + Vite frontend, ReactFlow for canvas rendering with Zustand state management, shadcn/ui as internal design system with wrapper pattern, Docker containers for backend services managed via full-path CLI commands or Docker socket API, IPC bridge between frontend and Rust commands for system operations.

stack_confidence.tauri_2: HIGH - Multiple production apps (Firezone, Dockerman, Hopp) validate the framework
stack_confidence.react_flow: HIGH - Well-documented performance patterns, Zustand integration is natural
stack_confidence.shadcn_ui: MEDIUM-HIGH - Excellent for custom design systems but requires governance discipline
stack_confidence.docker_management: MEDIUM - Works but requires careful implementation (full paths, lifecycle management)
stack_confidence.vite_build: HIGH - First-class Tauri integration, fast HMR, good production optimization
